title: Tracking Money Habits
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: What simple tracking method (notebook, app, or SMS review) can you start using this week?
Which spending pattern would you most like to understand better by tracking for 30 days?

stages.prompt: Why is tracking small daily expenses useful?

options.label: It shows where small leaks are happening
options.reflection: Correct. Many people are surprised by how much small, repeated expenses add up over a month.

options.label: It only wastes time and does not change anything
options.reflection: Tracking by itself is simple, and it often changes behaviour because you become more aware of each decision.

options.label: It is only useful for people with very high income
options.reflection: In fact, tracking is especially powerful when income is limited, because every rupee matters more.

options.label: Your memory is always enough to know where money goes
options.reflection: Most people underestimate how much they forget. Writing or recording gives a clearer picture than memory alone.

stages.prompt: Which is a simple and practical way to start tracking?

options.label: Write each expense in a small notebook or notes app
options.reflection: Yes. Even a very simple record builds awareness and helps you see patterns in a few weeks.

options.label: Check expenses only once a year during tax time
options.reflection: Once a year is too late to correct habits. Tracking needs to be regular to guide daily decisions.

options.label: Delete bank/SMS alerts so you don’t think about money
options.reflection: Avoiding information does not protect you. SMS alerts are useful records you can review.

options.label: Guess where money went at the end of the month
options.reflection: Guessing usually hides small but important leaks. Real numbers are more reliable than guesses.
